question: Substitute the appropriate values of the dummy variables in model 3 (above) to obtain the equations of the three response lines in Figure 12.23.
answer:
The complete model that characterizes the three lines in Figure 12.23 is

$$
E(y)=\beta_0+\beta_1 x_1+\beta_2 x_2+\beta_3 x_3+\beta_4 x_1 x_2+\beta_5 x_1 x_3
$$

where

$$
\begin{aligned}
& x_1=\text { advertising expenditure } \\
& x_2= \begin{cases}1 & \text { if radio medium } \\
0 & \text { if not }\end{cases} \\
& x_3= \begin{cases}1 & \text { if television medium } \\
0 & \text { if not }\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$


Examining the coding, you can see that $x_2=x_3=0$ when the advertising medium is newspaper. Substituting these values into the expression for $E(y)$, we obtain the newspaper medium line:

$$
E(y)=\beta_0+\beta_1 x_1+\beta_2(0)+\beta_3(0)+\beta_4 x_1(0)+\beta_5 x_1(0)=\beta_0+\beta_1 x_1
$$


Similarly, we substitute the appropriate values of $x_2$ and $x_3$ into the expression for $E(y)$ to obtain the radio medium line $\left(x_2=1, x_3=0\right)$ :

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(y) & =\beta_0+\beta_1 x_1+\beta_2(1)+\beta_3(0)+\beta_4 x_1(1)+\beta_5 x_1(0) \\
& =\overbrace{\left(\beta_0+\beta_2\right)}^{y \text {-intercept }}+\overbrace{\left(\beta_1+\beta_4\right)}^{\text {Slope }} x_1
\end{aligned}
$$

and the television medium line: $\left(x_2=0, x_3=1\right)$ :

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(y) & =\beta_0+\beta_1 x_1+\beta_2(0)+\beta_3(1)+\beta_4 x_1(0)+\beta_5 x_1(1) \\
& =\overbrace{\left(\beta_0+\beta_3\right)}^{y \text {-intercept }}+\overbrace{\left(\beta_1+\beta_5\right)}^{\text {Slope }} x_1
\end{aligned}
$$

meta_info.caption: Interpreting the $\beta$ 's in a Model with Mixed Independent Variables
meta_info.section: 12.8 Models with Both Quantitative and Qualitative Variables
